chunks.page_summary: This document provides a guide on how to configure Rheonics sensors with a LoRaWAN/Modbus TCP Master Converter (HD67D03-B2) and SenseCAP M2 gateway for remote data visualization via The Things Stack.
chunks.extracted_text:
Phone: +1 713 234 0863

Using LoRaWAN/Modbus TCP Master Converter for Remote Data Visualization of Rheonics Sensors

Modified on: Tue, 18 Nov, 2025 at 10:58 PM

What products are involved?
This article is based on using Rheonics Sensor Module Electronics (SME), SenseCAP M2 LoRaWAN gateway, and the HD67D03-B2 LoRaWAN/Modbus TCP Master Converter (868 MHz).

What is the purpose of this article?
This article describes the process of integrating Rheonics sensors with the HD67D03-B2 868MHz converter and the SenseCAP M2, so data can be transmitted wirelessly and visualized remotely.

TABLE OF CONTENTS

1. Overview
2. HD67D03-B2 868MHz Configuration
    2.1. Configuration in The Things Stack (TTS)
    2.2. Configure HD67D03-B2 with SW67D03 software
3. Sensor data visualization
4. References

1. Overview
LoRaWAN is a long-range, low-power communication protocol designed for transmitting sensor data over wide areas. In this article, the HD67D03-B2 converts data from the SME via Modbus TCP (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/folders/81000217062) and converts it into LoRaWAN messages. These messages are then sent to The Things Network (TTN) or other LoRaWAN network servers.
SenseCAP M2 gateway, which is connected to the Internet and sends the data to The Things Network (TTN) or other LoRaWAN network servers. TTN is a LoRaWAN ecosystem that includes The Things Stack (TTS), a LoRaWAN network server that enables remote data access. Once received, the data can be decoded and visualized remotely.
Following this setup, Rheonics sensors can transmit sensor values wirelessly and be monitored in real time from anywhere through The Things Stack server.

Figure 1: Architecture of Rheonics SME, HD67D03-B2, SenseCAP M2, and The Things Network integration

To configure the SenseCAP M2 LoRaWAN gateway refer to the following article: Configuring LoRaWAN Gateway for Remote Data Visualization of Rheonics Sensors (https://s...)

2. HD67D03-B2 868MHz Configuration

The HD67D03-B2 868MHz configuration needs to be done in The Things Stack and using the SW67D03 software.

2.1. Configuration in The Things Stack (TTS)

1. Enter The Things Network Console (https://console.cloud.thethings.network/) and access the account.
2. Select Create application.

Figure 2: Application creation
key_facts: This article provides instructions for integrating Rheonics sensors with the HD67D03-B2 868MHz converter and the SenseCAP M2 gateway for wireless data transmission and remote visualization.
LoRaWAN is a long-range, low-power communication protocol used for transmitting sensor data over wide areas.
The HD67D03-B2 converts data from the Rheonics Sensor Module Electronics (SME) via Modbus TCP into LoRaWAN messages.
The SenseCAP M2 gateway connects to the Internet and forwards data to The Things Network (TTN) or other LoRaWAN network servers.
The Things Network (TTN) is a LoRaWAN ecosystem that includes The Things Stack (TTS), which enables remote data access, decoding, and visualization.
Rheonics sensors can transmit values wirelessly and be monitored in real time from anywhere through The Things Stack server with this setup.
Configuration of the HD67D03-B2 868MHz requires using The Things Stack and the SW67D03 software.
diagram_labels: Rheonics Sensors (e.g., SRV, SRD, DVP, others) for density, viscosity, temperature

Figure 4: End device registration
5. Register the end device with the following parameters
Frequency plan: Select a frequency plan that matches the one specified in both the device and the SenseCAP M2 to ensure compatibility. In this case, Europe 863-870 MHZ (SF9 for RX2 - recommended)
LoRaWAN version: LoRaWAN Specification 1.0.1
Applications > srv > End devices > Register end device
Register end device

Europe 863-870 MHz (SF9 for RX2 - recommended)

LoRaWAN Specification 1.0.1
Regional Parameters version ? *
TS001 Technical Specification 1.0.1
Show advanced activation, LoRaWAN class and cluster settings
Figure 5: End device configuration
6. In the Provisioning information section, fill with the following and click on Register end device.
JoinEUI: e.g. 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 - This identifies the Join Server, which handles the initial communication between the end device and TTS. It is usually assigned by the device manufacturer, but it can also be set by the user. If no JoinEUI is provided, a value of all zeros can be used if the Join Server supports it. It is possible to use the same JoinEUI for different end devices, but the DevEUI should be unique per end device.
DevEUI: from HD67D03-B2 label
AppKey: generate a random key (necessary for the HD67D03-B2 to be able to connect to TTS)
key_facts: Applications allow users to register and manage end devices and their network data, and to pass this data to external services via integration options.
The frequency plan selected for an end device must match both the device and the SenseCAP M2 to ensure compatibility.
JoinEUI identifies the Join Server responsible for handling initial communication between the end device and The Things Stack (TTS).
JoinEUI is typically assigned by the device manufacturer, but users can also set it.
A JoinEUI value of all zeros can be used if the Join Server supports it when no specific JoinEUI is provided.
The same JoinEUI can be used for multiple end devices, but the DevEUI must be unique for each end device.
A random AppKey needs to be generated for the HD67D03-B2 device to connect to The Things Stack (TTS).
diagram_labels: Select the end device in the LoRaWAN Device Repository
Show advanced activation, LoRaWAN class and cluster settings
warnings_notes: The DevEUI should be unique per end device.
An AppKey is necessary for the HD67D03-B2 to be able to connect to TTS.

2.2. Configure HD67D03-B2 with SW67D03 software
1. Download the HD67D03-B2 software that can be found in SW67D03 software (https://www.adfweb.com/home/download/download.asp)
2. Connect the HD67D03-B2 through Ethernet to a PC as shown in the following diagram.
Figure 7: HD67D03-B2 connection to PC
3. Open the HD67D03-B2 software, which is under the name Compositor SW67D03.

The SW67D03 software can be downloaded from adfweb.com.
The HD67D03-B2 device connects to a PC via Ethernet for configuration.
The SW67D03 software includes steps for setting communication, LoRaWAN access, Modbus access, and updating device UDP.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides a step-by-step guide for configuring a SW67D03 Modbus TCP Master / LoRaWAN converter, including creating a new configuration, setting communication parameters, and defining LoRaWAN and Modbus TCP Master settings.
chunks.extracted_text:
4. Choose New Configuration and select a name for the configuration
   Create New Configuration
   SW67D03
   Create New Configuration
   Name of the new Configuration
   Rheonics
   OK
   Cancel
   Figure 9: New configuration
5. Select Set Communication
   ADFweb.com - Configurator SW67D03 Modbus TCP Master / LoRaWAN
   SW67D03
   Modbus TCP Master / LoRaWAN - Converter
   Begin

   www.ADFweb.com
   Figure 10: Set Communication
6. Fill the following spaces:
   * Ethernet Connection
     * Device name: viscometer
   * LoRaWAN
     * RF Band: EU868 (needs to be the same as the one assigned in the SenseCapM2 and TTS)
     * Network Type: Public
     * Adaptive DataRate: [checked box]
     * DataRate: 3 -LoRa: SF9/125kHz 1760 [bit/s] (make sure it is the same as in the Frequency Plan selected in TTS)
     * Transmission Type: Confirmed
     * Max Payload Size: 115
     * JoinEUI: 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 (Same JoinEUI as in the TTS end device creation)
     * Activation Method: Over-the-Air Activation (OTAA)
     * Application Key: Same AppKey generated in TTS end device creation
     * Port Field: Fixed
     * LNCHECK every xx min: 5
   * Modbus TCP Master
     * IP Address: 192.168.100.63 (The IP that is assigned to the HD67D03-B2 and used for uploading its configuration)
     * SubNet Mask: 255.255.255.0
key_facts: The SW67D03 is a Modbus TCP Master / LoRaWAN converter.
The configuration process involves creating a new configuration, selecting communication settings, and defining LoRaWAN and Modbus TCP parameters.
The RF Band for LoRaWAN must match the one assigned in the SenseCapM2 and TTS.
The LoRaWAN DataRate must match the Frequency Plan selected in TTS.
The JoinEUI for LoRaWAN must be the same as used in the TTS end device creation.
The Application Key for LoRaWAN must be the same AppKey generated in TTS end device creation.
The Modbus TCP Master IP Address (192.168.100.63) is assigned to the HD67D03-B2 and used for uploading its configuration.
diagram_labels: ADFweb.com - Configurator SW67D03 Modbus TCP Master / LoRaWAN
Opened Configuration of the Converter : Rheonics
warnings_notes: RF Band: EU868 (needs to be the same as the one assigned in the SenseCapM2 and TTS)
DataRate: 3 -LoRa: SF9/125kHz 1760 [bit/s] (make sure it is the same as in the Frequency Plan selected in TTS)
JoinEUI: 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 (Same JoinEUI as in the TTS end device creation)
Application Key: Same AppKey generated in TTS end device creation
IP Address: 192.168.100.63 (The IP that is assigned to the HD67D03-B2 and used for uploading its configuration)

8. Fill the spaces as follows
Start Byte: 1 - Position in the device's internal memory where data reading begins.
Num. Byte: 113 - Number of consecutive bytes of data to read starting from the Start Byte.
On Timer: (checkbox checked) - Enables sending the messages cyclically
Time(s): 30 - Sends messages every 30 seconds (or according to the interval specified).
key_facts: The SW67D03 device can be configured for Ethernet, LoRaWAN, and Modbus TCP Master communication.
The device hostname is set to 'viscometer'.
LoRaWAN settings include RF Band 'EU868', 'Public' network type, 'Adaptive DataRate', and 'Over-the-Air Activation [OTAA]'.
The LoRaWAN configuration specifies a maximum payload size of 115 bytes.
The Modbus TCP Master settings are configured with an IP Address of 192.168.100.63, a SubNet Mask of 255.255.255.0, and a Gateway IP of 192.168.0.1.
Data transmission for LoRaWAN can be enabled cyclically via an 'On Timer' setting, sending messages every 30 seconds.
The LoRaWAN data reading starts at byte 1 and reads 113 consecutive bytes.

10. The following table should be completed as follows to read the 23 parameters from Rheonics SME
*   SlaveIP Address: IP of the SME
*   Port: 502 (default)
*   Slave ID: 255
*   Type: Input Register
*   Address: Address of Modbus TCP Input Registers (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297664-modbus-tcp-input-registers)
*   NPoint: 2 - Number of consecutive registers to be read
*   Poll Time: 3000 ms
*   Position: Minimum position is 1, because the first byte is ignored. Between each position should be 4 bytes.

Figure 15: Modbus Set Access - Configuration

key_facts: The SW67D03 software acts as a Modbus TCP Master / LoRaWAN - Converter.
Configuration for LoRaWAN access involves setting start byte, number of bytes, and timer settings.
The Modbus Set Access configuration is used to read 23 parameters from a Rheonics SME.
The Modbus TCP configuration uses a default port of 502 and a slave ID of 255.
Modbus input registers are read with 2 consecutive registers per poll, occurring every 3000 ms.
The minimum position for reading Modbus data is 1, as the first byte is ignored.
A 4-byte interval is required between each Modbus position.
An example Slave IP Address of 192.168.100.62 is used in the Modbus configuration table.
diagram_labels: ADFweb.com - Configurator SW67D03 Modbus TCP Master / LoRaWAN
warnings_notes: Minimum position is 1, because the first byte is ignored.
Between each position should be 4 bytes.
Refer to Modbus TCP Input Registers documentation at: https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297664-modbus-tcp-input-registers

To address a limitation with Slave ID, users are instructed to navigate to the software's project folder.
Rheonics-related projects are located within the 'Projects' directory, specifically in a subfolder named 'Rheonics'.
The 'ModbusTCPM_SetAccess' file is used to configure and write 23 specific parameters.
warnings_notes: The SW67D03 software does not allow Slave ID = 255.

key_facts: The document provides configuration settings for a PROFINET_Master_to_Modbus_TCP_Master communication, specifically for Modbus_Read operations.
All listed Modbus Read operations use the IP address 192.168.100.62, Port 502, and Slave ID 255.
The configuration specifies 'Input Register' type for all Modbus read operations, with NPoint=2 and a PollTime of 3000 units.
The software version related to the Modbus configuration is 1.007.
The SW67D03 is identified as a Modbus TCP Master / LoRaWAN converter.
The document instructs the user to click 'Update Device UDP' if the IP address of the HD67D03-B2 is known.
The configuration tool for the SW67D03 converter is provided by ADFweb.com.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides step-by-step instructions for updating firmware and configuring the network IP of an SW67D03 device, including procedures for both known and unknown IP addresses.
chunks.extracted_text:
15. Insert the known IP, select configuration, and Execute Update Firmware.
    Figure 19: Execute Update Firmware
      Window Title: Update Device by Ethernet (UDP)
      Device Model: SW67D03
      Header: Update Device Using the Ethernet Port
      Input Field: Insert the IP Address of Device (192.168.100.63)
      Dropdown Label: Select Update Options
      Dropdown Value: Configuration
      Checkbox: Read Back (checked)
      Button: Cancel
      Button: Execute Update Firmware
16. An Ok message should appear.
    Figure 20: Ok message confirmation
      Window Title: ADFweb.com - SW67D03 Ethernet Update
      Version: Ver. 1.702
      Status: INIT : OK
      Status: FIRMWARE : Not Used
      Status: PROJECT : OK
17. If the IP of the device is unknown, turn OFF the device, and put Dip1 of Dip-Switch of the device in ON position. Turn on the device, connect the Ethernet cable to the PC, and insert the IP “192.168.2.205”.
    Figure 21: Update Device by Ethernet with default IP
      Window Title: Update Device by Ethernet (UDP)
      Device Model: SW67D03
      Header: Update Device Using the Ethernet Port
      Input Field: Insert the IP Address of Device (192.168.2.205)
      Dropdown Label: Select Update Options
      Dropdown Value: Configuration
      Checkbox: Read Back (checked)
      Button: Cancel
      Button: Execute Update Firmware
18. After the Ok message appears, turn OFF the device.
    Figure 22: Ok message confirmation
      Window Title: ADFweb.com - SW67D03 Ethernet Update
      Version: Ver. 1.702
      Status: INIT : OK
      Status: FIRMWARE : Not Used
      Status: PROJECT : OK
19. Put Dip1 of Dip-Switch A in OFF position and turn ON the device. The device can now be configured using the IP chosen in Set Communication configuration.
20. Connect the HD67D03 to the SME as the following diagram.
key_facts: Firmware updates and IP configuration for the SW67D03 device are performed using an Ethernet connection and a software utility.
For a known device IP, the user inserts the IP address and executes the 'Update Firmware' process.
If the device's IP is unknown, the device must be turned off, Dip1 of its Dip-Switch set to ON, then turned back on, and a default IP of '192.168.2.205' used for the update process.
Successful completion of the update or configuration is indicated by an 'Ok message' in the software, showing 'INIT : OK' and 'PROJECT : OK' statuses.
After an 'Ok message' appears and the update/configuration is complete, the device should be turned OFF.
To enable configuration using a newly chosen IP, Dip1 of Dip-Switch A must be set to the OFF position, and the device turned ON.
diagram_labels: Update Device Using the Ethernet Port
Insert the IP Address of Device
Figure 21: Update Device by Ethernet with default IP

chunks.page_summary: This page displays a JavaScript function for decoding sensor uplink data into various physical parameters and illustrates its configuration within a Modbus converter application's payload formatters section.
chunks.extracted_text:
function decodeUplink(input) {
  let bytes = input.bytes.slice(2);
  let data = {};
  const parameterNames = [
    "viscosity_median",      // Parameter 0
    "density_median",        // Parameter 1
    "temperature_median",    // Parameter 2
    "kinematic_viscosity",   // Parameter 3
    "density_average",       // Parameter 4
    "viscosity_raw",         // Parameter 5
    "density_raw",           // Parameter 6
    "temperature_raw",       // Parameter 7
    "resonant_frequency",    // Parameter 8
    "compensated_resonant_frequency", // Parameter 9
    "damping_frequency",     // Parameter 10
    "coil_temperature",      // Parameter 11
    "viscosity_last_good",   // Parameter 12
    "density_last_good",     // Parameter 13
    "modbus_512_display",    // Parameter 14
    "modbus_514_display",    // Parameter 15
    "modbus_516_display",    // Parameter 16
    "estimated_temperature", // Parameter 17
    "temperature_PT1000",    // Parameter 18
    "viscosity_model_calc",  // Parameter 19
    "density_model_calc",    // Parameter 20
    "concentration_model_calc", // Parameter 21
    "sensor_cleanliness_ratio" // Parameter 22
  ];

  function bytesToFloat32(byteArray) {
    let buffer = new ArrayBuffer(4);
    let view = new DataView(buffer);
    byteArray.forEach((b, i) => view.setUint8(i, b));
    return view.getFloat32(0, false);
  }

  for (let i = 0; i < bytes.length; i += 4) {
    if (i / 4 < parameterNames.length) {
      let paramName = parameterNames[i / 4];
      let floatValue = bytesToFloat32(bytes.slice(i, i + 4));
      data[paramName] = isNaN(floatValue)
        ? "NaN"
        : parseFloat(floatValue.toFixed(6));
    }
  }

  return {
    data: data,
    warnings: [],
    errors: []
  };
}

UI Screenshot Details:
Path: Applications > srv > End devices > modbus-converter > Payload formatters > Uplink
Navigation Tabs: Device overview, Live data, Messaging, Location, Payload formatters, Settings
Direction Tabs: Uplink, Downlink
Setup Section:

  Formatter code: (Partial view of the `decodeUplink` function)
    1 function decodeUplink(input) {
    2   let bytes = input.bytes.slice(2); // Ignore the first 2 bytes
    3   let data = {};
    4   const parameterNames = [
    5     "viscosity_median",      // Parameter 0
    6     "density_median",
    7     "temperature_median",
    8     "kinematic_viscosity",
    9     "density_average",
    10    "viscosity_raw",
    11    "density_raw",
    12    "temperature_raw",
    13    "resonant_frequency",
    14    "compensated_resonant_frequency",
    15    "damping_frequency",
    16    "coil_temperature",
    17    "viscosity_last_good",
    18    "density_last_good",
    19    "modbus_512_display",
    20    "modbus_514_display",
    21    "modbus_516_display",
    22    "estimated_temperature",
    23    "temperature_PT1000",
    24    "viscosity_model_calc",
    25    "density_model_calc",
key_facts: The JavaScript function `decodeUplink` is designed to process raw byte data from a sensor and convert it into meaningful physical parameters.

The function extracts 23 specific sensor parameters, including various viscosity, density, and temperature measurements (e.g., median, raw, average, kinematic), as well as Modbus-related display values and a sensor cleanliness ratio.
It initiates by discarding the first two bytes of the input data.
A helper function, `bytesToFloat32`, converts four-byte segments into 32-bit floating-point numbers.
The decoding logic iterates through the byte array, converting groups of four bytes into float values and assigning them to named parameters.
The function handles potential 'Not a Number' (NaN) results during float conversion by storing the string 'NaN' and formats valid float values to six decimal places.
This code is intended to be used as a 'Custom javascript formatter' within a 'Payload formatters' configuration interface, likely for a 'modbus-converter' application.
The screenshot demonstrates where this code snippet would be entered and configured in a user interface, indicating its role in data interpretation for uplink messages.
diagram_labels: Applications > srv > End devices > modbus-converter > Payload formatters > Uplink
Figure 26: Code in Payload formatters section

key_facts: To visualize parameters from Rheonics SME sensors, users must select the 'Live Data' tab in the web interface.
The displayed data includes sensor readings for compensated resonant frequency, damping frequency, density, temperature, and kinematic viscosity.
The system uses LoRaWAN for remote data visualization, with data processed by a 'modbus-converter' device.
The provided references offer extensive documentation on configuring LoRaWAN gateways, Modbus TCP, using The Things Network Console, and activating end devices.
